author_headline: Make something people want.
content:
TrustAI (YC S26) builds continuous compliance and governance for AI agents on sensitive systems.

After finding critical vulnerabilities in SAP's core AI agents, MIT engineers Hannah Chung and Medha Venkatapathy realized that legacy standards like SOC 2 and ISO 27001 were not enough to verify safety in AI tools. So, they built TrustAI to provide agents with appropriate governance.

They run evals across six domains: Data Privacy, Hallucinations, Permission Compliance, Robustness at Scale, Accountability, and Security. The result is a complete, transparent record on potential risks, suggested mitigative solutions, and time/cost efficiency analysis.

Medha did LLM inference-optimization research with Jacob Andreas at MIT CSAIL and was on the US Physics Olympiad Team. Hannah did econ research at the World Bank and quant at Virtu Financial.

Considering or running an agent on sensitive systems? Building one that connects to other enterprises' private data? Book a demo at trytrust.ai or email hello@trytrust.ai.

Congrats on the launch, Hannah and Medha!

🚀 https://lnkd.in/gt9wq29S

You are struggling because of this 1 reason 🔻

→ You're still preparing like it's 2020.

Back then, solving random DSA questions for hours was enough to stand out.
Today, thousands of candidates have completed the same sheets.

So, instead of solving 500 random questions, you need to:
→ Master patterns instead of memorizing solutions.
→ Review your mistakes until they become strengths.
→ Learn to explain your thinking, not just write code.
→ Build projects that can demonstrate your skills.

The hiring bar has changed and your preparation should change, too.

author_headline: Making Finance Less Complicated | CFA L1 Candidate | McKinsey Forward | Helping Founders & Brands Build Their Voice Through Content & Personal Branding | 4.7M+ Impressions | 70+ Brand Partnerships
content:
I went from being a competitive exam failure to...

Building a 16,000+ person community on LinkedIn.

And honestly?

The journey wasn't as glamorous as the numbers make it look.

A few years ago, I was that person who had a plan.

A competitive exam.
A dream.
A timeline.

But things didn't go according to the plan.

I failed.

And for a while, it felt like everyone was moving ahead except me.

Friends were getting jobs.
People around me were achieving milestones.

And I was stuck questioning:

"Am I falling behind?"

But sometimes, failure doesn't close a door.

It forces you to build a different one.

I started writing.
Not because I had figured everything out.

But because I was learning, and I wanted to share what I was learning.

One post became ten.
Ten became hundreds.

Slowly, LinkedIn became more than a platform.

It became a place where I could:

→ Simplify finance for students trying to understand money
→ Share lessons from my own journey
→ Help brands and founders tell their stories better
→ Build meaningful conversations with people I may never meet otherwise

Today, I'm grateful to have:

• Completed my Master's with 2nd rank in uni
• Built an organic community of 16K+ people on LinkedIn
• Created content with and for brands & founders
• Continued my journey of upskilling in finance every single day

But the biggest lesson wasn't about followers.

It was this:

Your biggest setback can become the foundation of your biggest comeback.

You don't need to have everything figured out to start.

You just need the courage to take the next step.

Because growth rarely happens overnight.

It happens quietly.

One skill.
One conversation.
One post.
One day at a time.

Still learning.
Still building.
Still becoming. ✨

What's one "failure" from your past that secretly shaped who you are today?

Would love to hear your story. 👇

author_headline: Founder @ ProPeers | Ex-Amazon | Ex-Founding Member at Coding Ninjas | Youtuber(80k+) | DTU
content:
I still remember the day I got my first stipend from Amazon. After 2-3 years of hustling in college, it's a great feeling to see your hardwork generate results.

But at the same time, it is very easy to feel lost when you are still in the "before" part of the story.

Your classmates might be posting offer letters while you are still refreshing the careers page.

Your friend might be buying a new phone from internship money while you are thinking twice before ordering Swiggy.

Your junior might crack a big brand while you are still explaining to relatives why you do not have a package yet.

Your parents might be quietly worrying about fees while you are wondering if all this DSA-Grind is even worth it.

It feels like life is moving for everyone else, and you are stuck on the loading screen.

Yaar, sach bolun to ye phase sabse heavy hota hai.

Here is what I wish someone had told me then:

Skill compounding is invisible in real time.

Those nights solving questions, building small projects, fixing bugs that nobody will ever see, they do not pay immediately. They raise your skill level.

The first stipend, the first offer, the first 6-digit balance in the account, that is not the start of your hard work. That is just the first time the world pays you for what you have been building in silence.

If you are still in the phase where money is tight and results are slow, it does not mean you are worthless or incapable. It means you are still on the path where most people give up.

Keep learning. Keep applying.
Ask for help. Take breaks without quitting.

One day that "dream amount" will land in your bank, and you will know in your gut:

This is for me.
For my hard work.
And I earned every rupee of it.

What will people remember about you years from now?

Career achievements, awards, and milestones may open doors, but they are not what define your legacy.

People remember the moments when you made them feel valued, respected, and supported.

The way you treat others during your journey leaves a deeper impression than any title or accomplishment.

Chase success, but never forget kindness. Make your impact on the people the achievement you are most proud of.

author_headline: Software Engineer | DSA | TypeScript | Python | SQL | Docker | Redis | System Design
content:
If someone had told me back then that I'd graduate as a COLLEGE TOPPER with an off campus offer, receive 4+ job offers and complete 3 internships I wouldn't have believed them.

Four years ago, when I first walked into college, I had no idea what could I possibly do in this stream.

The biggest turning point wasn't a course or a placement. It was the people I met during my second year. Looking back, that year shaped the rest of my journey.

My first internship in Pune in September 2024 gave me my first experience of real world software development. I later interned at Betatest Solutions and Hummingbird, and each experience helped me grow as an engineer and as a person.

Because of these internships, I spent less time on campus than I expected. But I never felt like I missed out. The friends, mentors, teammates, and everyone I met along the way made these years unforgettable.

Graduating as the class topper for all 8 consecutive semesters and college topper is something I'm truly proud of. Not because of the title, but because it reminds me what consistency can achieve.

Thank you to everyone who has been part of this journey. Every conversation, challenge, late night debugging session, and piece of advice helped me get here.

IES College of Technology Bhopal
Prince Chaurasia Keshav Maheshwari

author_headline: SDE-2 @Servicenow |Top 1% linkedin | Top Marketing Voice | 332K+ @LinkedIn | Backend, Springboot, Android, C++, Java, Javascript, AWS | Competitive Programmer..
content:
One story can inspire you.

A pattern makes you stop and think.

Yesterday, I came across a reel celebrating Ashi Goyal, who secured AIR 29 in NEET UG 2026

It reminded me of something I've noticed year after year.

Every result season introduces us to students with completely different stories.

A student aspiring to become the first doctor in the family.

A student like Suraj, whose dream stayed alive because ALLEN's Shiksha Sambal initiative removed financial barriers.

A student like Abhishek Kumar Agrawal, who rebuilt his journey after unimaginable personal loss with the support of his family and ALLEN's mentors.

And then there are the top ranks—AIR 1, AIR 2, AIR 4, AIR 7, AIR 9, AIR 29.

Different backgrounds.

Different challenges.

Different journeys.

Yet one name consistently appears alongside them.

ALLEN.

Looking at this year's results—5 students in the Top 10 and 47 students in the Top 100—it's difficult to believe this is just about individual brilliance.

It reflects an ecosystem.

One that identifies potential, builds strong academic foundations, provides structured mentorship, removes financial barriers where needed, and helps students perform at their best.

Great institutions aren't remembered because they produce one extraordinary student.

They're remembered because they help thousands of ordinary students achieve extraordinary outcomes.

That's what makes a result remarkable. And that's what makes an ecosystem enduring.

author_headline: SDE 2 @amazon | Top 1% Career Coach, Topmate
content:
Growing from SDE → SDE-2 at Amazon changed one thing about how I approach interviews.👇

The biggest jump wasn't learning another algorithm.
→ It was learning to drive the discussion after the solution.

I've noticed many engineers stop once they've reached the optimal complexity.

Strong SDE-2 candidates usually start asking questions like:

🔹Can this data structure survive changing requirements?
Today's requirement is fast lookups (HashMap). Tomorrow it's ordered queries (TreeMap) or Top-K (Heap). Explain why your choice scales with the problem, not just today's input.

🔹Where's the real bottleneck?
Don't stop at O(n). If profiling shows JSON serialization, repeated object creation, or network I/O dominates the request, that's where optimization effort belongs.

🔹What's the first production issue this solution could create?
Would concurrent updates require optimistic locking? Could retries create duplicate writes without idempotency? Would caching (Redis) improve latency but introduce stale reads?

Those are the discussions that separate "I can code" from "I can own this in production."

That's the mindset I gradually built while growing at Amazon, and it's exactly what stronger SDE-2/SDE-3 interviews evaluate.

That's also why Bosscoder Academy focuses on more than just solving DSA questions. Through structured mentorship, production-oriented System Design, and mock interviews, you learn how to reason like an engineer, not just code like one.

If you're aiming for a switch to SDE-2/SDE-3 roles while balancing a full-time job, I'd highly recommend getting structured guidance from the industry experts at Bosscoder Academy.

🔗 Explore their program here: bcalinks.com/8UmKnOs

They've helped 2200+ engineers crack top PBC roles through:

💡 Structured curriculum to help you master DSA, System Design, & AI
💡 Real world, AI-powered projects & 24/7 doubt-solving
💡 1:1 mentorship from industry experts
💡 Resume reviews, mock interviews, & job switch support

#collab #softwareengineering #amazon #sde2 #systemdesign #interviewprep #careergrowth

author_headline: B.Com (Hons.) @ DU'27 | Personal Branding • Storytelling • Finance | Brand Partnerships
content:
Today, I paid ₹ 20 for my printouts.

Strange how the same ₹ 20 tells two different stories.

I walked into a printing shop today.

Five minutes later, I walked out with my documents.

Nothing unusual.

But while leaving, I noticed something I had never thought about before.

📜 A student walked in to print an assignment.
📜 A man came to photocopy a few documents.
📜 Someone asked for passport-size photos.
📜 Another customer wanted a single page printed.

Each person spent only a few rupees.

Each stayed for barely five minutes.

And yet...

That tiny shop existed because hundreds of strangers chose to trust it with small things.

Not once.

But every single day.

It made me wonder how many businesses around us are built exactly like this.

Not through one massive sale.

But through thousands of ordinary moments.

🌸 One customer.
🌸 One receipt.
🌸 One recommendation.
🌸 One return visit.

We often celebrate businesses when they become brands.

We rarely appreciate them when they're still earning trust one customer at a time.

Maybe that's why small businesses feel so personal.

They're not built on transactions.

They're built on tiny moments of trust repeated over and over again.

Sometimes, the strongest foundations aren't built all at once.

They're built five minutes at a time.

#Storytelling #SmallBusiness #Business #Perspective #Entrepreneurship
